videos.title: Grand Fondo Come Back - Ready or Not?
videos.description:
Two years ago, after a 30-year break from cycling, I rode 7.6 km. In a few days, I’ll take on Amy’s Gran Fondo — 122 km and nearly 1,900 meters of climbing. At 70, it’s the toughest ride of my life.
In this episode — the first in the Gran Fondo Series — I take you through my final prep:
✔️ Bike setup
✔️ Fuel and gear
✔️ Training
✔️ Ride strategy
I’ve been documenting my journey back to fitness here on YouTube — and this is the ride it’s all been leading to. If you're returning to cycling, training for something big, or just looking for a bit of motivation — you're in the right place.
Subscribe to follow the series and see how the ride unfolds.
Leave a comment if you’ve taken on a challenge that pushed you further than you thought possible.
Let’s ride this one together.

Gran Fondo Series https://youtube.com/playlist?list=PLwO8Y3sW-0fxmoeQd4_f4REoCtSqMGMsd&si=vSq-RCxrC0hHbnxs

https://www.amysgranfondo.org.au
https://roadcyclingacademy.com 
https://www.cycplus.com/

Affiliate Link
CYCPLUS 5% Off https://www.cycplus.com/?ref=kiwkrgml


Links
 
Strava
strava.com/athletes/5967790

This content was made using 
iPhone 13 and Insta 360 x3
FulGaz
DaVinci Resolve
MidJourney
Pexels

Chapters
00:00 Intro
00:41 The Bike
04:42 Training & Preparation
07:13 Fuelling
09:15 Strategy
11:11 Share your strategies & tricks
videos.transcript:
On August the 2nd, two years ago, after
a 30-year break from cycling, I rode 7.6
km. 2 days later, I followed that up
with 16 km, and I've been pushing my
limits ever since. In a few days, I'll
be riding in Amy's Grand Fondo, 122 km
with nearly 1,900 m of elevation. At 70,
this is my toughest ride ever. Over the
past few weeks, I've been documenting my
progress here on YouTube as I prepare
for this ride. Today I'll be talking
about the bike training and preparation,
what I'll be taking with me, and the
strategy to get through to the end. What
could this ride and the moon have in
common? Stick with me and I'll explain.
But first, let's head outdoors and check
out the bike.
This is a Fuji Grand Fondo 2.1 made in
2018. I bought it secondhand. It's just
been serviced at the bike shop and it's
looking good. Lovely and clean as well.
So, it's got endurance geometry. It's
more relaxed, which really suits an
older rider like me with less
flexibility. It's size 58. It's kind of
extra large carbon carbon frame. So,
originally it had Shimano Altegra
components, but it was swapped out
before I bought it with Jura Ace. It has
compact chain rings. The rear cassette
was 1128. I swapped that out to uh 1134
because
I just knew I couldn't get up those
hills on 1128. So then I had to go back
to uh Altegra for the cassette and
Altegra long cage uh rear derailia. I've
had to replace the rear wheel. It's a
Shimano wheel. The the front wheels are
the um Fuji branded oval. Uh, it's got
28 mm Grand Prix
uh 5000 tires and TPU tubes. I'm using
hybrid uh pedals because I just feel
more comfortable with them. Particularly
with the shoes, I'm often stopping stop
stopping starting on bike paths and I
found with the the road shoes and the
hard plastic cleats that my foot might
slip or did slip occasionally and I had
a bit more trouble getting out of the
pedals. This is easier. And also, if I
get to a really steep hill and I'm
worried about not being able to clip out
and I'm going like 3 kilometers an hour
before I get to that point, I can put my
foot on the flat part of the pedal and
uh just easily take it off if I need to.
I've added a power meter which is on the
other side. That's uh Altegra. Uh I have
heart rate monitor which is paired up to
the Wahoo Element Bolt version two bike
computer. Cypus sent me this uh
electronic bike pump. Dogs just come
along and licked it a moment ago.
So Cypus AS2 Altegra bike pump. I think
this works really well. It does get hot
at first and I thought this nice grippy
case uh was really lovely, but it's
actually to do with the insulation
because yeah, it does get hot. So I've
used this pump a couple of times out on
the road. It's very slow. takes a long
time to get up to any sort of decent
pressure and I don't think I've even got
to 60 PSI. This unit though is much
quicker and it will get me to the full
60 or 80 PSI. So, my 28 mm tires take
about 90 seconds to get up to 80 PSI and
it has enough power to use it twice. It
also has an adapter for straighter
valves and um a little um needle which
is for pumping up balls, that sort of
thing. And this isn't a waterproof case
because the unit itself isn't
waterproof, but it looks good, feels
good. It seems to recharge pretty
quickly. Not that I timed it, but the
being the person that I am, I'd probably
charge it each time I charge my bike
computer or more often. So, my saddle
bag, I have try to keep it really small.
I don't like big things on my bike, but
I have $20,
uh, a tool multi-tool kit, some tire
levers,
um, a spare tube,
and a spare um, snack bar, a spare
battery for my
crank and for my for my power meter and
for my heart rate. I did change them uh,
a week ago, just to be sure.
I'll also be taking an Insta 360 X3
camera with a microphone and my iPhone.
As Lance Armstrong wrote, it's not about
the bike. Well, it's not just about the
bike, but what follows is equally or
arguably more important. Let's head back
inside.
In a previous video, I talked about the
difference between training and
preparation. Training is the work you do
on the bike. Prep is everything that
supports that work off the bike. Rest,
sleep, recovery, nutrition, and mental
readiness. One focus for me this time
has been getting enough protein. It's
something I hadn't really monitored
before. While I don't weigh every gram,
I make sure there's protein in every
meal and in snacks, things like eggs,
legumes, and nuts. Recently, I actually
gained some weight, not a lot, when I
thought I'd be losing it. It's a tricky
balance. Fueling properly while trying
to stay lean and strong.
I've been doing a 60-second plank most
days. I'm impressed with many of you
though, running, hiking, and gym work.
I gave myself a 10e lead in to train
specifically for Amy's. Before that, it
was all about building endurance, easy
rides, no hills, just staying consistent
and keeping the legs turning over. That
gave me a solid foundation once I ramped
things up. My goal has been to ride at
least three times a week. Some weeks
it's only two, others I managed four. In
one week recently, I totaled 8 and a
half hours riding and covered over 180
kilometers while aiming for consistency
rather than perfection. One big
challenge are the hills. I live and
train mostly in flat areas. To work
around that, I made trips to Yara
Boulevard and tied in a ride in the
Dandong Hills with a talk I was giving
nearby. That turned out to be my first
proper hill test, and I mostly held my
own. The real game changer was buying a
smart trainer. Over the past few weeks,
Melbourne spring weather has thrown
everything at us. Gusty winds, wet
roads, and unpredictable conditions.
Riding outdoors in those conditions
means getting pulled out of my training
zone constantly. Hills, stop starts, and
battling crosswinds and headwinds. It's
hard to stay focused. But the trainer,
that's precision. Indoors, I can ride to
power. Choose the gradient and build
fitness without interruption.
>> I think it said about 800 meters
elevation. That's going to kill me.
So, I'm just going to shut up and try
and get through this.
>> It used to feel like cheating, like I
wasn't doing real riding. But now I see
it differently. Letting go of my
snobbery about indoor training has been
one of the best lessons of this whole
preparation. But it doesn't replace time
spent outdoors. Fitness is only part of
the equation. Let's look at what I'll
take with me to keep riding for 7 hours.
Everything I've read and every video
I've watched points to consuming 60 to
70 gram of carbs per hour during
endurance rides. I've done some
experimenting on and off the bike, and
here's the approach I've settled on. My
main source of energy is a powder mix
called Staminate.
It also has sodium and potassium, which
is helpful when it's warm, plus table
sugar. That's it. No fancy gels or
products.
One 970 mil bottle with this mix gives
me over 80 gram of carbs. And for solid
food, I pair that with fruit cake from
my local supermarket.
It's small, compact, and it gives me
roughly 52 g of carbs. It's sweet, but
that's what's needed, and it's not
expensive. And I can refresh the pallet
with a cold beer at the end.
I can get over 60 gram of carbs per hour
from half a bidden of the drink mix and
a half piece of fruit cake and around
500 mls of water per hour to stay
hydrated. I'll top up extra carbs and
water at the aid stations which have
water hydration products lollies fruit
and even more fruit cake if I'm not
already sick of it.
I'll have extra drink mix I'll have in a
ziploc bags.
Fruit cake will be pre-cut and
individually wrapped and easy to access
so I can eat without stopping.
Everything goes in my jersey pockets,
but I may bring a top tube bag. I'll
decide once I start packing. I've set a
reminder on the bike computer. Every 20
minutes, eat drink because it's easy to
forget. Ryan Thomas at Road Cycling
Academy says that once you get behind on
your carb intake, you can't catch up.
And I've done that before. You can never
catch up on fueling, but you can be
prepared.
>> My phone also goes in my jersey pocket.
Oh, and I'll have sunscreen with me. All
of that, the food, the hydration, the
reminders is only part of the picture
because fueling well means nothing if I
don't pace myself smartly. That's where
strategy comes in.
This is where all the preparation comes
together. Amy's grand fondo isn't just
about the bike, the training, or
fitness. It's about strategy. And just
as much, it's about the mind. A phrase I
came across recently stuck with me. Ride
the first 90 minutes easy, the last
minutes proud. That's the goal. In the
past, I've made the classic mistake
riding at a tempo that feels fine early
on, especially when I'm surrounded by
faster riders. But 90 minutes, 2 hours
in, I'd hit a wall and I wouldn't
recover. And the thing is, I'm not
competitive. In the moment, I get
carried away. When I was younger, I
could push through. These days, it's a
battle between what feels good and what
gets me to the finish line without
falling apart and a sobbing mess. That's
where pacing and planning come in. There
are four refreshment stops on the
course, each about 90 minutes apart at
my pace. Sounds great, but each has a
cut off time.
If I average 17 km an hour, I'll scrape
through, but that doesn't account for
stopping. To be safe, I need to maintain
closer to 20 kilometers an hour, which
is what I average on my longer rides.
But on the climbs, of which there are
many, I'm slow. I'm 6'1, 90 kilos. My
average speed drops sharply. While I do
make up time on descents, I descend
cautiously, so the gains are modest.
The plan is to maintain a steady,
sustainable pace, watch my heart rate,
and ignore faster riders. If I'm feeling
good, I might skip the first stop and
ride straight through to Forest. That's
50 kilometers in. Then pause a bit
longer there to recover before the
biggest climb of the day, Mount Sabine.
I rode this section on the Smart
Trainer, and it was brutal. Last year, I
took the SAG wagon. This year, it's my
unfinished business. If you have
thoughts on strategies and tricks, let
me know in the comments.
Other strategies at the refreshment
stops, refill bottles, grab one food
item, and roll. Just a few minutes at
each stop. Mount Sabine, climb, shift
early, keep heart rate down, and find a
rhythm. A tip I've been given is for a
small dose of caffeine, 60 to 80
milligrams before the second climb,
which helps to keep focus, reduces
perceived exertion. Caffeine can also be
helpful towards the end of the ride when
mental fatigue creeps in. After 4 hours,
expect my heart rate to drift slightly
higher. Digestion slows. Sticking with
liquid carbs and easier fuels like
lollies and bananas. Keep solid food
small and frequent. Avoid big chunks.
With this strategy, pacing, and a bit of
grit, I plan to ride it through. If
you're riding Amy's, get in touch. It
would be great to meet up beforehand.
This is just one part of the Grand Fondo
series. Check out the others to see how
it all comes together. Why go through
all this? To borrow from JFK's moonshot
speech, I don't do it because it's easy.
I do it because it's hard. At this stage
of life, pushing my limits has given me
something wonderful. Focus, purpose, and
a challenge worth showing up for. Cheers
until next time.
videos.curation_reason: Recent personal preparation video detailing the specific challenge of 122km with 1,900m elevation at this gran fondo.

videos.title: 2024 Gran Fondo Asheville
videos.description:
🚴‍♂️ Over 600 riders conquered iconic sections of the Gran Fondo Asheville 2024, including Elk Mountain, the French Broad River, and Grapevine Road! 🌞 Despite hot but manageable temperatures of 93 degrees, the spirit and energy of the cyclists shone brightly.

🏆 2024 Gran Fondo Asheville Results:
Men’s Granfondo
1st: Carlos Julian Quintero, Delray Beach FL - 1:05:46
2nd: Jeff Case, Pelzer SC - +0:36.45
3rd: Andrew Knight, Marietta GA - +0:39.53

Women’s Granfondo
1st: Jennifer Thornton-Brooks, Boone NC - 1:17:49
2nd: Carly George, Tampa FL - +4:11
3rd: Valentina Cummings, Asheville NC - +10:25

👉 Don't miss out! Sign up for the Boone Gran Fondo on August 4th! Let's ride! 🚴‍♀️

https://www.granfondonationalseries.com/gran-fondo-boone

videos.title: The Gran Fondo Badlands
videos.description: The Gran Fondo Badlands is an amazingly challenging and beautiful ride through the hoodoos and valleys of Dinosaur Land, Drumheller, Alberta. The varying distances available to ride make it an event for all level of cyclists.

quotes.text: In a few days, I'll be riding in Amy's Grand Fondo, 122 km with nearly 1,900 m of elevation. At 70, this is my toughest ride ever.

gear_mentions.text: Swapped out 11-28 rear cassette to 11-34 because couldn't get up those hills on 11-28. Had to go back to Ultegra for the cassette and Ultegra long cage rear derailleur.
Using 28mm Grand Prix 5000 tires with TPU tubes.

Using hybrid pedals instead of road shoes and hard plastic cleats because foot might slip and had trouble getting out of the pedals. Can put foot on flat part of pedal and easily take it off if needed on really steep hills.

race_day_tips.text: Electronic bike pump gets hot during use and is very slow on the road, takes a long time to get up to decent pressure and may not even reach 60 PSI.
Changed power meter and heart rate monitor batteries a week before the race just to be sure.

additional_quotes.text: At 70, this is my toughest ride ever. 122 km with nearly 1,900 m of elevation.
Race is going great it's hot out but it's wonderful, awesome group going fast going hard, stoke is high everybody's having a good time.

rider_intel.search_text: Riders describe the Boone Gran Fondo as a challenging 122km route with nearly 1,900 meters of elevation gain. The terrain requires specific gearing considerations, with one 70-year-old rider needing to swap from an 11-28 to 11-34 cassette to handle the hills. Race conditions can be hot during the event. Equipment recommendations include 28mm tires for the course. Riders report the climbing difficulty requires careful preparation, with some using hybrid pedals for easier foot placement on steep sections when going very slow speeds. The race attracts riders of various ages and abilities, with participants describing a supportive community atmosphere despite the physical demands. Electronic pumps may struggle to reach adequate pressure on course repairs. Proper preparation includes battery changes for electronic components before race day.
